coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence/electrochemiluminescence approach for instant detection of glycated hemoglobin index
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Dongning
Fang, Chen
Li, Huiling
Tu, Yifeng
coredata.dc:description: The percentage of glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) in total hemoglobin (Hb) is an important index for the diagnosis of Type II diabetes (T2D) because it reflects the long-term glucose level in blood. Herein, employing a one-pot co-reduction approach using glutathione (GSH) as structure-directing agent, a cluster-like AuAg nanoparticle (AuAg NPs) material was synthesized, therefore an electrochemiluminescence (ECL) aptamer-sensor for HbA1c detection was developed based on functionalized electrode with this material. Meanwhile, the quantitative determination of total Hb was realized based on the quenching effect of Hb on the fluorescence (FL) of luminol. Under compatible conditions, the results of both indexes can be satisfactorily acquired. This multimodal detection system has a good linear response toward Hb from 0.1 to 2.5 μM and HbA1c from 0.005 to 0.5 μM. The blood test proves this strategy is capable of accurate Hb and HbA1c detection, thus to obtain the percentage of HbA1c in total Hb (HbA1c%), which has the potential application for clinical diagnosis of diabetes mellitus.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Glycated hemoglobin
Aptasensor
AuAg nanoparticles
Fluorescence
Electrochemiluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the preparation and consummation of aptasensor
The verification of Hb detection by luminol-FL
The multimodal analysis of Hb and HbA1c
The feasibility of multimodal analysis for clinical diagnosis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM images of AuAg NPs with (A) low-, (B) medium-, and (C) high-resolution. (D) The corresponding SAED pattern of AuAg NPs. Inset in (A) shows the distribution of particle-size.
The (A) HAADF-STEM image, (B, C, D) HAADF-STEM-EDS mappings, (E) line scanning profiles and (F) EDS spectrum of AuAg NPs.
(A) The XRD spectra of AuAg NPs and standard Au (JPCDS-04-0784) and Ag (JPCDS-04–0783). The XPS (B) survey spectrum and high-resolution spectra of (C) Au 4f, (D) Ag 3d of AuAg NPs.

(A) The SERS spectra of AuAg NPs/ITO, aptamer/ITO and aptamer/AuAg NPs/ITO. The (B) CV and (C) EIS curves in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM of Fe(CN)6 3− (inset in (C) is the Randles equivalent circuit); (D) The CV images in PBS containing 5 × 10−5 M of luminol, both for (a) ITO, (b) APTMS/ITO, (c) AuAg NPs/APTMS/ITO and (d) resultant aptasensor.

Fig. 5(A) The ECL curves of 5 × 10−5 M luminol on different electrodes in PBS. (B) The excitation spectrum (black, λem = 435 nm) and emission spectra (red, λex = 300 nm; blue, λex = 357 nm) of luminol. (C) The effect of pH on the FL and ECL performance of luminol. (D) The FL of luminol at different concentration of Hb, inserted is the regression of relative quenching degree upon CHb (0.1–2.5 μM). (E) The ECL response of the aptasensor upon HbA1c concentration with calibration as inset one. (F) The interference of 100 μM coexisting substances as UA, Glu, Na+, Mg2+, ALB and Ins for 0.1 μM Hb or HbA1c by FL or ECL. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The principle of the detection system.
Comparison of recent methods for the determination of HbA1c.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *: N/A: Not available in the study.

*: 4-MPBA: 4-mercaptophenylboronic acid; Au NFs: Au nanoflowers; SPCE: Screen-printed carbon electrode; GNs: Graphene nanosheets; FTO: Fluorine doped tinoxide; CS: Chitosan; rGO: Reduced graphene oxide; DPV: differential pulse voltammetry MGCE: magnetic glassy carbon electrode.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of HbA1c content in real samples.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence/electrochemiluminescence approach for instant detection of glycated hemoglobin index

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Material Science, Soochow University, Suzhou, 215123, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Material Science, Soochow University, Suzhou, 215123, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Endocrinology, The Second Affiliated Hospital of Soochow University, Suzhou, 215004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Second Affiliated Hospital of Soochow University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Endocrinology, The Second Affiliated Hospital of Soochow University, Suzhou, 215004, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The First Affiliated Hospital, Nursing College, Soochow University, Suzhou, 215006, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The First Affiliated Hospital, Nursing College, Soochow University, Suzhou, 215006, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The percentage of glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) in total hemoglobin (Hb) is an important index for the diagnosis of Type II diabetes (T2D) because it reflects the long-term glucose level in blood. Herein, employing a one-pot co-reduction approach using glutathione (GSH) as structure-directing agent, a cluster-like AuAg nanoparticle (AuAg NPs) material was synthesized, therefore an electrochemiluminescence (ECL) aptamer-sensor for HbA1c detection was developed based on functionalized electrode with this material. Meanwhile, the quantitative determination of total Hb was realized based on the quenching effect of Hb on the fluorescence (FL) of luminol. Under compatible conditions, the results of both indexes can be satisfactorily acquired. This multimodal detection system has a good linear response toward Hb from 0.1 to 2.5 μM and HbA1c from 0.005 to 0.5 μM. The blood test proves this strategy is capable of accurate Hb and HbA1c detection, thus to obtain the percentage of HbA1c in total Hb (HbA1c%), which has the potential application for clinical diagnosis of diabetes mellitus.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: For decades, a great deal of effort has been made to discover novel mechanism and concrete measures to develop accurate chemo/biosensors, substrate materials, and instruments for the detection of biomarkers with high sensitivity, specificity and reproducibility [1,2]. Some analytical strategies are also established as the foundation for multimodal detection of several indicators or their conversion value of a certain disease.

Diabetes mellitus is one of the most common chronic disease worldwide. The detection of blood glucose by enzymatic or nonenzymatic methods is relatively mature and has been applied as a principal index for its clinical diagnosis and domestic management [3]. However, glucose concentration in blood is easy to be affected by meal, medicine and daily activity, thus it is in large fluctuation within a day. Hemoglobin (Hb), is one of the main protein components in red blood cell, contains four polypeptide subunits (two α- and two β-globins). Non-covalently bound to each one of those subunits, there are heme groups which undertook the function of carrying oxygen [4,5]. Hb can form glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) by nucleophilic attack from aldehyde group of glucose to one or both of its N-terminal valine under high glucose level [6]. The produced HbA1c would persist in blood steadily for about 120 days, thus the percentage index of HbA1c in total Hb (HbA1c%) could reflect long-term blood sugar level more accurately [7]. Accounted for about 4.0–6.0% of total Hb in healthy human blood, HbA1c gets to be a primary methodology for diagnosing diabetes nowadays [8]. The American Diabetes Association (ADA) recommended a threshold level of 6.5% as the criterion for the diagnosis of diabetes in 2010 [9], also the World Health Organization (WHO) recommended HbA1c of 6.5% as the cut point for diagnosing diabetes in their Use of Glycated Haemoglobin (HbA1c) in the Diagnosis of Diabetes Mellitus at 2011 [10]. In accordance, it is also included into the supplementary diagnostic criteria for diabetes in China. So, multimodal, sensitive and precise detection of Hb and HbA1c is of great importance for clinical diabetes diagnosis.

Recently, beside the classic technology as high performance liquid ion chromatography for clinical detection of HbA1c index, the methods including enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), surface plasmon resonance (SPR), fluorescent or electrochemical ones were also attempted [11,12]. Faced with this proposition, there will be momentous challenges to distinguish and ultra-sensitively detect the HbA1c in total Hb through multimodal detection, because of not only the much lower content of HbA1c than Hb in blood, but also the complexity of blood components, and the structural similarity of dual targets which might result in mutual interference.

Fluorescence (FL) is a sensitive, accurate and rapid mean for bioanalysis. Label-free fluorescence was widely applied for Hb detection based on its unique optical and biochemical properties [5]. For recognition of Hb in FL, three ways are generally adoptable as: 1) using the inner filter effect (IFE) of Hb to quench FL [13]; 2) making use of covalent coordination or interaction between the ferrous ions and fluorescent material (containing imidazolium), and the Fenton reaction to improve the quench effect [14]; and 3) the fluorescence resonance energy transfer between Hb and FL material [15]. Herein, simple and inexpensive FL probe is greatly aspired for the application.

Researchers had also put much effort into the detection of HbA1c, but some imperfections still existed such as intricate pretreatment and separation process, the interference between detection signals, and so on. Aimed on the saccharified terminal of HbA1c, a serious of electrochemical, chromatographic and spectral methods based on immune interaction, boronate affinity or enzymic catalysis (fructosyl amino-acid oxidase, FAO) have been reported for HbA1c detection [16,17]. Different from those, aptamer has specific sequence and certain three-dimensional structure, which can recognize target sensitively and selectively. Using aptamer as bioactive recognizing host to construct the biosensor, the aptasensor has advantages of low cost, good biocompatibility, high sensitivity and specificity compared to other types of sensors [18]. For example, Chang et al. successfully developed an integrated microfluidic device using aptamer-based testing approach to measure HbA1c in blood sample [19]. Also there were works focused on simultaneous detection of Hb and HbA1c, for example, Moon and co-workers have developed an amperometric dual-sensor for Hb and HbA1c determination [20]; Hossain et al. have achieved simultaneous detection of Hb and HbA1c using an electrochemical AC field modulated separation channel coupled with an electrochemical sensor [4].

Luminol and its derivatives have been widely-used as the luminescent probe for fluorescence, chemiluminescence (CL) and electrochemiluminescence (ECL) assay with the advantages of high luminescent efficiency, simple operation and low cost [21]. Luminol-ECL is widely applied in areas like clinic diagnosis, environmental monitoring and food safety based on its redox induced light-emission, has both the advantages of spectroscopy and electrochemical techniques [22,23]. But, as though a well-known FL probe, luminol is scarcely involved in bioanalytical applications in weak alkaline environment [24], even more not to be mentioned in the simultaneous detection of Hb and HbA1c.

AuAg nanoparticles (NPs) material has beneficial applications in biosensors because of its excellent biocompatibility, catalytic activity, electrochemical and optical properties [25]. Particularly valuable is that its property can be greatly regulated by its composition and structure, even subtle change in doped-metal has a dramatic effect on its properties. Studies have discovered that the electrochemical and optical properties of Au NPs were greatly improved after doping Ag to form AuAg bimetallic NPs [26]. Zhang and coworkers prepared Au@Ag nanorods (NRs) to fabricate a sensitive ECL immunosensor for carcinoembryonic antigen assay, by loading the corresponding antibody on it [27]. Various synthetic methods including chemical protocols, laser ablation, phase transfer, digestive ripening and electro-polymerization were employed for controllable preparation of AuAg NPs. Besides, bio-inducing method, using biomolecule as diverting agent, has advantages of relative simplicity, nontoxicity and cost-effectiveness, becoming a hotshot method recently [28]. Zamora Mendoza and coworkers have prepared a 3-D arrayed AgAu NPs using the extract of Aloysia triphylla as reducing and stabilizing agent [29]. In our previous works, a kind of tiny AuAg NPs was produced under the guidance of glutathione (GSH) [30], and another cluster-like AuAg hollow-NPs was obtained under the guidance of bovine serum albumin (BSA) taking NH2NH2 as reductant [31].

Inspired by these shining points, an advanced approach for multimodal detection of Hb and HbA1c is designed based on the luminescent property of luminol. The prepared cluster-like AuAg NPs using NH2NH2 as reducing agent under the guidance of GSH acts as the supporter to load mercaptized aptamer to develop the ECL aptasensor for HbA1c assay. The specific molecular recognizing/binding capacity of aptamer toward HbA1c results in increased steric hindrance for ECL probe-luminol, leading to the signal attenuation. Meanwhile, the quantitative determination of total Hb is carried out by the quenching degree of luminol FL arose out of the static binding between luminol and Hb. Thus, the glycated hemoglobin index can be acquired instantly from them with single sample in single setup.
The chemicals and instruments required in the experiments are listed in the Supplementary Material. The sequence and secondary structure of the HbA1c aptamer are shown in Fig. S1.

AuAg NPs were prepared by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method. Briefly, 2 mL of GSH (20 mM), 1.8 mL of HAuCl4 (20 mM) and 0.2 mL of AgNO3 (20 mM) were mixed into 8.0 mL of water, under stirring at ambient temperature, 0.1 mL of NH2NH2 was injected into the solution drop by drop (generally 5 drops). Continuous stirring for 0.5 h after the color of mixed solution got to dark brown, the material was centrifugal washed at 7500 rpm with water for several times and finally collected, then re-dispersed into water to totally 3.0 mL and stored in a fridge at 4 °C.

The detailed pretreating process of basal ITO electrode was described in SM. 50 μL of 0.05 mg/mL AuAg NPs was dropped on APTMS functioned ITO, resting for 3 h at room temperature. By the addition of 0.28 mg of TCEP in 1 mL of buffered aptamer solution (1 × 10−6 M) and saved in dark for 1 h to break the disulfide bridge between aptamer molecules [32], 10 μL of this solution was coated on the surface of AuAg NPs and incubated for 4 h at 25 °C. After rinsed with phosphate buffer (0.2 M, pH = 8), the electrode was immersed in 2% BSA solution for 1 h to block those residual active sites to finally accomplish the sensor. After thoroughly rushed with PBS solution, it was stored at 4 °C for further use.

The determination of Hb or HbA1c was both conducted in PBS (pH = 8) containing 5 × 10−5 M of luminol. For FL detection of Hb, FL excitation wavelength is set at 357 nm, the widths of excitation and emission v band-slits are both 5 nm. The FL signal at 435 nm was captured and recorded when Hb sample was added into buffer solution. For ECL detection of HbA1c, 10 μL of HbA1c sample solution was dropped on the aptasensor, placed at room temperature for ample time to achieve a full incubation, washed with PBS solution and blew with N2, then to test the ECL response in abovementioned buffer solution.

In the ECL sensing process, conventional three-electrode system was adopted, consisting of Ag wire as reference electrode, Pt wire as counter electrode, with a pulsed voltage (limited at −0.3 and 1.2 V, and period of 4 s). The recorded signal is the average of ten successive peaks of ECL pulse.

The morphology of as-prepared NPs was firstly observed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM). Fig. 1 shows those well-distributed spherical particles with the mean diameter of 36.4 nm (A), and the rough surface cluster-like structure (B). The high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1C) shows the distinct lattice fringes of particle, there the inter-planar spacing distances are measured to be 0.235 and 0.204 nm, matched well with the low index planes (111) and (200) of the AuAg alloy. Meanwhile, the selected-area electron diffraction (SAED, Fig. 1D) pattern illustrates the polycrystalline nature of AuAg NPs [33].

The high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) mappings and line scanning profiles are used to visually illustrate the composition distribution and structural features of the AuAg NPs (Fig. 2 A–E). Au and Ag both distribute throughout the whole particle, demonstrating a homogeneous alloy structure. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 2F, the atomic molar ratio of Au to Ag measured by energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) analysis is 91.1 to 8.9, matched well with the feed ratio of the metal precursors (9:1), verifying the full reduction of two precursors.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was applied to reveal the crystallinity of the particle, taking bulk Au and Ag (JCPDS: 04–0784 and 04–0783) as references. As displayed in Fig. 3 A, five diffraction peaks at 38.0°, 43.8°, 64.8°, 77.5° and 81.6°, correspond to the fcc crystal planes of (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222), respectively [34].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is powerful to analyze the surface valence and chemical composition of AuAg NPs [35]. The survey XPS spectrum of product (Fig. 3B) shows weak signals at about 162, 285 and 400 eV beside the peaks of Au and Ag, reveals the existence of S, C and N from the GSH. From the correlated high-resolution Au 4f spectrum (Fig. 3C), there is a pair of specific peaks of metallic Au0 at 83.7 and 87.4 eV. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Ag (Fig. 3D) also evidences the form of Ag0, by two strong peaks emerged at 367.6 and 373.6 eV, assigned to metallic Ag 3d5/2 and 3d3/2.

By regulating the dosage of GSH, it has dominating effect on the morphology of NPs as structure-directing agent, the formation mechanism of AuAg NPs can be further revealed. Along with gradually increasing GSH concentration from 0.5 mM to 3 mM, larger particle size and denser core were achieved, meanwhile the surface of particles become smoother, transformed to a cluster-like shape from the floral pattern (Fig. S2). In addition to this, too little GSH does not guarantee the effective dispersion of NPs, but too much GSH hinders the anisotropic growth of AuAg core. Thus, based on the LaMer curve, the possible formation mechanism of AuAg NPs can be illustrated. First of all, the metal precursors bonded with GSH via –SH and other groups (i.e. carboxy groups) to form the coordination complex, thereafter they were reduced to form Au and Ag atoms while the addition of NH2NH2. These GSH capped atoms will quickly fused together to get a minimized interface free energy to form the AuAg nuclei, and then undertake the anisotropic and epitaxial growth under the guide of GSH, according to the coalescence mechanism [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the preparation and consummation of aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: As known, AuAg NPs has excellent plasmonic property and is widely used as Surface Enhancement Raman Scattering (SERS) substrate material. There are a lot of SERS-based aptasensors had been developed for the applications in bioanalysis and food safety analysis [37]. Hence, the preparation process of aptasensor might be investigated using the Raman spectrum. As shown in Fig. 4 A, compared to AuAg NPs, there is not any characteristic Raman peak can be seen, weak peaks can be found at 307, 597, 1084 and 1242 cm−1, assigned to vibrational modes of purines and pyrimidines of aptamer [38,39]. And then the enhanced and blue-shifted (about 10 cm−1) characteristic Raman peaks of aptamer emerged, meanwhile a new peak at 275 cm−1 appeared, can be attributed to Au–S conjugation between the NPs and aptamer, indicating the aptamer was assembled on AuAg NPs. These results indicating the successful connection of aptamer on sensor surface [40].

Electrochemical techniques as cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed to validate the construction of the aptasensor. As show in Fig. 4B, there were all a couple of distinct reversible redox peaks on ITO, APTMS/ITO, AuAg NPs/APTMS/ITO and resultant aptasensor in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3− at scan rate of 50 mV s−1. The peak current gets maximum on AuAg NPs/APTMS/ITO, implying the feasible and flexible electron transfer between NPs and Fe(CN)6 3−. To be sure, the loading of APTMS and aptamer would hinder the electron transfer, led to decreased current. Fig. 4C is the Nyquist plots of different electrodes, the experimental data can be fitted to Randles equivalent circuit (R(Q(RW)) by Zsimpwin software. The equivalent circuit consists of the solution resistance (Rs), Warburg impedance (Zw), double layer capacitance (Cdl) and the apparent charge transfer resistance (Ret). Similarly, the smallest semicircle were appeared after loading AuAg NPs, indicating its excellent conductivity with lowest Re t (14 Ω), smaller than bare ITO (26 Ω). Not surprisingly, the resistances increased significantly when APTMS or aptamer was attached onto the electrode surface (36 Ω, 57 Ω respectively). These electrochemical tests also reveal the successful preparation of aptasensor.

The alteration of surface morphology in preparation process of the sensor was also characterized using an atomic force microscope (AFM), as shown in Fig. S3. The Ra of ITO and APTMS/ITO is 3.86 and 3.13 nm respectively, APTMS is like a squashy membrane loading on the surface of ITO, reduces its roughness. In contrast, the Ra of AuAg NPs/APTMS/ITO is 17.9 nm because of the granularity of NPs. The Ra of resultant sensor is lower than it as 11.7 nm, indicating that the successful connection of aptamer filled part of the gaps between NPs due to the molecular flexibility and viscosity.

The CV curves in Fig. 4D display the redox performance of 5 × 10−5 M luminol on electrodes in different stages and the final aptasensor in PBS solution. On AuAg NPs/APTMS/ITO, there is a reversible redox cycle of AuAg NPs from 0.20 to 0.68 V, it will catalyze the electrochemical oxidation of luminol to give markedly increased oxidation current compared to bare ITO or APTMS/ITO after 0.92 V. This is the main causation of ECL enhancement of luminol on functionalized electrode thereafter aptasensor. On the other hand, cluster-like structure of NPs offered a rough surface, which enlarged the effective surface area of electrode for 1.19-fold (0.74 cm2/0.62 cm2) and to be sure exposed more catalytic active sites (measured by Randles-Sevcik equation, Fig. S4) [41]. The excellent electrocatalytic activity of AuAg NPs was also embodied in the ECL test further, as shown in Fig. 5 A. The ECL intensity of luminol on AuAg NPs/APTMS/ITO is 10.8 times higher than that on bare ITO in PBS solution. After incubating with aptamer and BSA, the ECL background of obtained sensor is moderate and stable.

To obtain the best detection performance of ECL aptasensor for HbA1c, the preparation process of sensor was optimized. The thickness and density of AuAg NPs on electrode surface has profound effect on the property of aptasensor. Using same volume, the ECL intensity of luminol on sensor reached to highest at the content of AuAg NPs as 0.05 mg/mL but decreased sharply at higher content (Fig. S5). APTMS plays a role of cross-linking agent, too little could not load plenty NPs but too much APTMS would hinder the electron transfer, thus 0.03% is the optimal concentration (Figs. S6A and 10 μL of solution was used). To come true the specificity of sensor, it is necessary to block residual active sites to avoid nonspecific binding. Along with the increasing content of sealing agent BSA, ECL signal on resultant sensor kept reduced until 2% (Fig. S6B), because of the poor conductivity of BSA which hindered the electron transfer [42], thereafter the signal got stable, reflected the saturation of BSA on sensor surface.

Moreover, the electrochemical parameters, like the pulsed voltage and period, also have direct relation to the ECL performance of luminol. The ECL intensity got maximum when the lower limiting potential of −0.3 V, upper limit potential of 1.2 V, and the pulse period of 4 s (Figs. S6C and S6D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The verification of Hb detection by luminol-FL
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence is similar to chemiluminescence, except the difference of excitation energy [43]. The results showed in Fig. 5B reveal the FL property of luminol to verify its feasibility for FL detection of Hb. Luminol has two excitation wavelengths at 300 and 357 nm in PBS solution, and an emission at wavelength of 435 nm (with 4.46 ns of the decay time in PBS solution, Fig. S7, measured by Phase Modulation Method). The effect of pH on the FL intensity was examined, as shown in Fig. 5C. At 357 nm of λex, the FL intensity of luminol at 435 nm was decreased obviously along with the increasing pH, totally contrary to the ECL performance. Thus, for the coadaptation of multimodal detection, pH 8.0 was chosen as the optimal condition for test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The multimodal analysis of Hb and HbA1c

ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 illustrates the detection principle. Here, originated from certain static interaction between luminol and Hb (proteins like Hb, Mb, BSA etc all incline to bind with small molecules as luminol) via various covalent or noncovalent binding, including electrostatic force, hydrogen bonding, etc, to form ground-state complexes, which lead to FL quenching of luminol [44,45].

The HbA1c assay is based on the impedance effect upon affinity interaction of HbA1c on aptasensor. Aptamer can specifically recognize target molecule based on spatial conformation, thereafter to form the stable conjugation via hydrogen bond and/or Van der Waals force [46]. The HbA1c specific aptamer has a low Kd of 7.3 nM, showing high affinity to HbA1c. As the result, the specific binding of HbA1c onto aptamer leads to the steric resistance which hinders the mass and electron transfer of ECL probe [19]. This response mechanism induces the decline of luminol ECL, thus the ECL intensity on sensor inversely correlates to the HbA1c concentration.

Fig. 5D is the fluorescence emission spectra of luminol in PBS solution (pH = 8.0) with the addition of different concentrations of Hb, the emission wavelength red shifted and intensity reduced continually. The relative fluorescence quenching degree (ΔI/I0) exhibits good linear regression vs. Hb ranged in the concentration from 0.10 to 2.5 μM (inset in Fig. 5D, R2 = 0.997), the linear equation is y = 0.256x + 0.0193 and the detection limit is calculated to be 0.03 μM (S/N = 3).

The test results evidenced that the Hb would not cause noise in ECL detection, the recorded emission signal of luminol on aptasensor almost negligible went from 0.033 to 0.035 (only 0.13% of background) after adding 1 × 10−5 M of Hb (Fig. S8), ensures the independence and accuracy of HbA1c determination. In Fig. 5E, from 0 to 1 μM, the ECL intensity decreased along with the concentration of HbA1c, the ΔI/I0 linearly regressed to it in the range from 0.005 to 0.5 μM with the equation y = 0.998x + 0.0278 (R2 = 0.997) and a detection limit of 1.45 nM (S/N = 3). This detection limit is low enough than the normal human HbA1c physiologic level (3–13 mg/mL) [12], beats the rivals of recent HbA1c electrochemical biosensors as shown in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: The feasibility of multimodal analysis for clinical diagnosis
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the feasibility of this approach for clinical diagnosis of diabetes, the capacity of anti-interference was tested. Fig. 5F shows the test results of common components (100 μM) of blood on the detection, including small bioactive molecules like uric acid (UA) and glucose (Glu), metal ions like Na+ and Mg2+, proteins like serum protein (ALB) and insulin (Ins), without significant disturbance to those detection signals (FL of 1 μM Hb and ECL of 1 μM HbA1c). During storage for 5 weeks, the signal of FL changed for 7.63% and ECL signal changed for 5.79% (Fig. S9), showing a good stability.

Three venous blood samples collected from diabetic patients in Second Affiliated Hospital of Soochow University were diluted 50-fold by PBS, after 0.5 h, they were centrifuged for 15 min (3400 rpm, 4 °C) to obtain isolated Hb which was released from red blood cells. 10 μL of this sample solution was dropped into the luminol solution directly for FL test, another 10 μL of 100-fold re-diluted solution was incubated on the aptasensor for ECL test. The HbA1c percentage index was calculated by the division of two results and checked with clinical standard (ion exchange chromatography). As listed in Table 2 , the accuracy (in relative error) between two methods is 6.67%, 6.25% and 7.14%, respectively, this is already a very good result.

In summary, a novel multimodal approach based on luminescent performance of luminol was designed to detect HbA1c percentage index of blood. The Hb detection was achieved by the quenching effect of Hb on luminol FL. The HbA1c detection came true through a novel ECL aptasensor built on AuAg NPs substrate. Under the compatible conditions for two targets, the proposed approach showed high sensitivity and specificity for HbA1c% detection, and the results of blood test demonstrated its feasibility and credibility for clinical diagnosis of diabetes. However, there is still an obstacle which encumbered the real application of this approach. For the reason of different sensitivity, the blood sample need different ratio of dilution to adapt it, thus it obstructs the implement of multimodal test in single setup temporarily. And, on other hand, to design and fabricate an installation enable to carry out multimodal test is in anticipation. These are the topics that need to be intensively studied in further research.

Dongning Li: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Chen Fang: Investigation. Huiling Li: Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Yifeng Tu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21675115, 21375091).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ab.2022.114958.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in ultrathin two-dimensional nanomaterials
Chem. Rev.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Au and Au-based nanomaterials: synthesis and recent progress in electrochemical sensor applications
Talanta
